worth_it_explanation: PMP and PG deals carry negotiated terms that should clear ahead of the open auction, so a deal that stalls or a floor that fights the open market trades away the value the deal was struck to protect. This check reads the deal configuration a publisher already runs through Index Exchange and its header-bidding wrapper, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a concrete deal ID or floor the publisher can act on.
intro: Index Exchange routes Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals from buyers into a publisher's header-bidding wrapper and ad server, where each deal is identified by a deal ID and carries its own floor and priority. This subcategory is about the operational health of those deals, whether the deal IDs are live and transacting and whether their floors and priorities are set so the deal clears in the order the publisher intends. These checks confirm that the deals a publisher negotiated are actually delivering rather than sitting idle, and that deal pricing does not undercut or get undercut by the open auction.

audit_checks.title: Deal health is not monitored, so stalled or idle deals go unnoticed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A negotiated PMP or PG deal represents committed buyer demand at an agreed price, so a deal ID that stops transacting is revenue the publisher expected and is no longer receiving. Without a regular read of deal-level delivery, a stalled deal ID, a mistargeted deal, or a buyer who has paused spend can sit idle for weeks before anyone notices, and the lost spend is rarely recovered after the fact.

educate.base:
A Private Marketplace (PMP) or Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal is an agreement struck ahead of the auction between a buyer and a publisher, identified by a deal ID that the buyer references when it bids. In a header-bidding setup, Index Exchange passes the deal and its deal ID through the wrapper to the ad server, where the publisher creates the line item that lets the deal clear. The deal represents demand the publisher has already negotiated, so the publisher reasonably expects it to deliver at the agreed terms.

The gap this check looks at is between a deal existing and a deal delivering. A deal ID can be active in the contract yet transact nothing, for reasons that do not announce themselves. The buyer may have paused or shifted budget, the deal may target inventory the publisher rarely serves, the line item may be misconfigured so the deal never wins, or the deal ID itself may have been entered incorrectly on one side. Each of these shows up the same way from the outside, as a deal that simply is not spending, and none of them surfaces unless someone reads delivery at the deal level.

Index Exchange and the header-bidding wrapper both report deal-level signals that make this visible. The wrapper exposes the winning deal ID through targeting values such as 'hb_deal' and 'hb_deal_BIDDERCODE', and the ad server records impressions and revenue against the deal's line item, so a publisher can read which deal IDs are transacting and which have gone quiet.

A few signs point to deal health going unmonitored:
  • No regular report breaks revenue and impressions out by deal ID, so idle deals are not visible
  • A deal ID that delivered in the past shows no recent impressions and no one has confirmed why
  • The publisher cannot say, for each active deal, whether it is currently transacting and at what pace
You may want to confirm that each active deal ID is delivering as expected. One approach is to build a deal-level delivery report on a regular cadence and flag any deal that goes idle for follow-up with the buyer or a configuration check.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, deals are often tied to specific sections or sponsorship-adjacent placements, so a deal can stall simply because the targeted section saw a traffic dip or a seasonal lull. You may want to read deal delivery alongside traffic to the targeted inventory, so a quiet deal can be traced to either a demand-side pause or a supply-side traffic change before raising it with the buyer.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals may target higher-intent product or category inventory that fluctuates with merchandising and seasonality. You may want to monitor deal delivery against the inventory each deal targets, so a deal that goes idle on product pages during a slow buying period is read in context rather than mistaken for a broken deal ID.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Server | Features | Deals for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Index Exchange's detailed configuration and deal-reporting documentation for this is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the list of active deal IDs against deal-level delivery to see which deals are transacting and which have gone idle.

wrapper_config.label: Header-bidding wrapper configuration
steps: List the active PMP and PG deal IDs configured for Index Exchange in the header-bidding wrapper, along with the buyer and the inventory each targets.
For each deal, note the 'hb_deal' or 'hb_deal_BIDDERCODE' targeting value the wrapper passes so the corresponding ad server line item can be matched.
Confirm each deal ID has a matching, active line item in the ad server so the deal can win when its bid arrives.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server and Index Exchange reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal ID, with impressions, revenue, and average price, over a recent window long enough to show a delivery trend.
Compare each active deal's recent delivery against its earlier delivery to see whether any deal has gone idle or dropped sharply.

For any deal showing no recent impressions, record whether the cause sits on the demand side (e.g., a paused buyer) or the supply side (e.g., a misconfigured line item or deal ID), based on the available signals.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each active deal ID, whether it is transacting and at what pace, and whether a regular deal-level delivery report exists. A setup where deal delivery is monitored on a cadence and every active deal is either delivering or has a recorded reason for being idle passes. A setup where no deal-level delivery read exists, or where idle deals sit unexplained, is a fail recorded with the deal IDs affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active deal ID and its recent delivery above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal delivery at the deal-ID level, so a stalled or idle deal can go unnoticed. A negotiated deal represents committed buyer demand at an agreed price, and when a deal ID stops transacting, whether from a paused buyer, a mistargeted deal, or a misconfigured line item, that expected revenue is lost until someone reads delivery and acts. {{voice_recommend}} building a deal-level delivery report on a regular cadence that breaks impressions and revenue out by deal ID, flagging any deal that goes idle for follow-up with the buyer or a configuration check. After the report is in place, review it on a set schedule so idle deals surface within days rather than weeks.

audit_checks.title: Deal floors and priorities are misaligned with the open market
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal is meant to clear ahead of the open auction at its negotiated price, so a deal floor set below the open-market floor lets an open bid undercut the deal, while a deal line item priced or prioritized below open-market line items lets the auction win impressions the deal should have taken. Either misalignment erodes the value the deal was negotiated to capture, on every impression the deal touches.

educate.base:
A deal carries two settings that decide where it sits relative to the open auction. The first is the deal floor, the minimum price the buyer agreed to pay for the deal, which travels with the deal ID in the OpenRTB Deal object. The second is the priority of the ad server line item that the deal clears through, which decides whether the deal is considered ahead of, alongside, or behind open-market line items. Both have to agree with the publisher's intent for the deal to deliver the value it was struck for.

When the deal floor and the open-market floor are out of step, the open auction can undercut the deal. If a deal floor sits below the floor a publisher applies to open-market demand, an open-market bid at the higher floor can outprice the deal and take the impression, even though the deal was negotiated to have first claim. The deal still exists, but the inventory it was meant to win flows to the open auction instead, at a price the publisher did not intend to favor.

Priority can pull the same direction. Prebid's guidance is that deal line items should sit at a higher priority than open-market line items, and typically below direct-sold inventory, so deals clear ahead of the auction but behind guaranteed direct campaigns. A deal line item left at the same priority as open-market line items, or below them, lets the open auction win impressions the deal should have taken, regardless of the deal price. The 'preferdeals' setting in the wrapper reinforces this by letting the highest-value deal win as the overall bid, but it cannot fix a line item priced or prioritized against the deal in the ad server.

A few signs point to deal floors and priorities being misaligned:
  • A deal floor sits below the open-market floor applied to the same inventory, so an open bid can outprice the deal
  • Deal line items share the same priority as open-market line items rather than sitting above them
  • Deals deliver fewer impressions than the buyer's pace suggests, while the open auction wins inventory the deal targets
You may want to confirm that each deal's floor and line-item priority place it ahead of the open auction as intended. One approach is to compare each deal floor against the open-market floor on the same inventory and check that deal line items carry a higher priority than open-market line items.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium sections often carry a higher open-market floor to protect direct-sold value, so a deal targeting those sections can be undercut if its negotiated floor was set below that premium floor. You may want to compare each deal floor against the open-market floor on the specific sections the deal targets, rather than against a site-wide average.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category inventory can carry higher open-market floors because the audience is closer to a purchase, so a deal on that inventory needs a floor and priority that hold up against the stronger open-market demand. You may want to check that deals targeting commercial pages are not undercut by the higher open-market floors those pages already apply.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Server | Features | Deals for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Index Exchange's detailed configuration documentation for deal floors and priorities is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each deal's floor against the open-market floor on the same inventory, and check that deal line items carry a higher priority than open-market line items.

wrapper_config.label: Header-bidding wrapper configuration
steps: List the active deal IDs for Index Exchange with the negotiated floor recorded for each deal.
Record the open-market floor the publisher applies to the same inventory each deal targets, so the two floors can be compared directly.
Confirm whether the 'preferdeals' setting is enabled in the wrapper, which lets the highest-value deal win as the overall bid.

ad_server_config.label: Ad server line item configuration
steps: For each deal, open its ad server line item and record its priority relative to the open-market line items.
Confirm deal line items sit at a higher priority than open-market line items and, where direct-sold campaigns exist, below those.
Flag any deal whose floor sits below the open-market floor on the same inventory, or whose line item shares or sits below open-market priority. Either one lets the auction undercut the deal.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per deal, the deal floor against the open-market floor on the same inventory and the deal line item's priority against open-market line items. A setup where each deal floor sits at or above the open-market floor and each deal line item carries a higher priority than open-market line items passes. A setup where a deal floor sits below the open-market floor, or a deal line item shares or sits below open-market priority, is a fail recorded with the deal IDs and the misaligned setting.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's floor against the open-market floor and its line-item priority above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more deals on {{site_name}} carry a floor or line-item priority that lets the open auction undercut them. A deal is meant to clear ahead of the open market at its negotiated price, so a deal floor set below the open-market floor, or a deal line item priced or prioritized at or below open-market line items, lets an open bid take inventory the deal should have won. {{voice_recommend}} comparing each deal floor against the open-market floor on the same inventory and raising any deal floor that sits below it, and setting deal line items to a higher priority than open-market line items while keeping them below any direct-sold campaigns. After the change, read deal delivery to confirm the deals clear the inventory they target rather than losing it to the auction.
